producer.name: Ahmad Tea
producer.history: British tea brand based in London (also operates Ahmad Tea USA); known for tin-caddy packaged loose-leaf and bagged teas distributed globally. Holds 22+ Great Taste Awards. 'English Tea No. 1' is one of their flagship blends: Ceylon (high-grown, central Sri Lankan hills) + Kenyan + Assam, with a light touch of bergamot. Ahmad Tea positions it explicitly as 'more fragrant than English Breakfast, less bergamot than Earl Grey' — a middle ground between the two classics.

brewing.notes: Per Ahmad Tea: 3-5 minutes infusion in boiling water; ~1 tsp per cup. Best served hot with a slice of lemon (per producer recommendation) or with a splash of milk. Single infusion.

notes:
Black teas keep well — stable urgency regardless of which
interpretation is correct.

producer.name: Mitsuyasu Seikaen
producer.history: Historic Fukuoka tea shop founded in 1716, currently in its 13th generation. Based in Hakata, specializes in Yame tea. Akishō-cha (秋賞茶) literally means 'autumn appreciation tea' — a curated seasonal selection by the shop's owner, intended to be enjoyed in autumn. SKU not publicly catalogued; details below are inferred from category context.

brewing.notes: APPROXIMATED — exact brewing parameters depend on the actual content of the seasonal blend. Treating as a sencha-style premium green: 70°C, 60s first infusion; 70°C, 15s second; 75°C, 45s third. Adjust based on actual leaf appearance: if the leaves look like asamushi (whole-needle), keep cool and short; if fukamushi (deep-steamed/broken), even shorter on first infusion.

notes:
Curated seasonal blend from Mitsuyasu Seikaen, themed around autumn
appreciation. The exact composition varies year-to-year as the shop
selects what's at peak quality for the season. Expected to be a
Yame-area sencha or kabusecha-style tea given the shop's focus.
Ask Francesco for any tasting notes from package or experience to
refine this entry.

name.primary: Anxi Monkey King (Ma Liu Mie) Tie Guan Yin Oolong Tea

producer.name: TeaVivre
producer.history: Sourced from the Fujing Tea Garden in Huqiu, one of the main Tie Guan Yin production areas in Anxi County, Fujian (500-800m elevation). 'Ma Liu Mie' is Hokkien for 'monkey-picked' — the name comes from a legend that the cliffs where the tea grows are too steep for human pickers, so monkeys were trained to climb them. Belongs to the zhengchao type of Tie Guan Yin. Anxi was named the Home of Chinese Oolong by the Ministry of Agriculture in 1995.

brewing.notes: Gongfu schedule (110ml gaiwan, 7g): rinse, 30s, 40s, 50s, 60s, 80s, 100s, 120s. Western: 5g per 355ml at 100°C, 3-5 minutes.

notes:
Lightly oxidized green-style Tie Guan Yin (qingxiang). Strong and
long-lasting orchid-like floral fragrance, smooth and brisk on the
palate, with a sweet aftertaste. Tightly rolled jade-green pearls
unfurl into whole leaves through multiple infusions.

producer.name: Ikeda Senchado
producer.history: Private brand of Ikeda Seicha Co. (founded 1948), based in Tenmonkan, Kagoshima. Run by tea master Kenta Ikeda (茶師十段, 10th-dan). This asamushi (light-steamed) sencha sources leaves from northern Satsuma (北薩) — the cooler, mistier mountainous part of Kagoshima — to bring out aromatic intensity and refined character through a short, gentle steaming.

brewing.notes: Asamushi schedule: 70°C, 60s first; 75°C, 10s second; 80°C, 30s third. Light-steamed senchas need slightly longer first infusion than fukamushi (the leaves are intact and need time to open). The liquor stays clear and pale even after multiple infusions — a hallmark of asamushi vs. fukamushi cloudiness.

notes:
Light-steamed sencha with whole needle-shaped leaves. Liquor is
pale and limpid; flavor is sharp, refined, and aromatic — distinct
from the cloudier, more concentrated character of deep-steamed
fukamushi. The northern Satsuma terroir adds mountain-misty depth.
A premium daily sencha that rewards careful brewing.

producer.name: Nagata Seicha
producer.history: Small family-run tea producer in Aso (Kumamoto, Kyushu), led by Kōji Nagata. Eco-Farmer certified (low-pesticide cultivation philosophy). Won 1st place in the steamed green tea category at the North American Tea Championship. Self-grown and self-processed — leaves picked and processed the same day, then vacuum-packed to preserve freshness. The Aso volcanic soil and high-altitude mist create a distinctive terroir for green tea.

brewing.notes: Premium sencha schedule: 70°C, 90s first; 75°C, 15s second; 80°C, 45s third. Cool the water (boil and pour through cooling vessels). 'Tokusen' grade rewards careful attention to temperature — too hot and the umami collapses into bitterness.

notes:
Premium 'tokusen' sencha from a small Aso family farm. Fine, balanced
flavor described by Nagata-san himself as 'delicate' — the producer
emphasizes year-over-year consistency as a craft. Award-winning at
North American Tea Championship (steamed green tea category, 1st
place). The same producer also makes a wakōcha (Japanese-style
black tea) using Aso no Megumi cultivar leaves.

name.primary: Award Winning Premium Dragon Well Long Jing Green Tea

producer.name: TeaVivre
producer.history: From the Long Jing Village Tea Garden in Xihu (West Lake), Hangzhou, Zhejiang — the original protected appellation for Xihu Long Jing. Uses the Longjing #43 cultivar, certified as a National Improved Variety in 1987 (GSCT 37). Long Jing was favored as imperial tribute by Emperor Shunzhi during the Qing Dynasty. The flat, smooth leaf shape is the result of pan-firing by hand against a hot wok.

brewing.notes: Gongfu schedule (110ml gaiwan, 4g): rinse, 20s, 30s, 40s, 60s. Western: 2g per 250ml at 85°C, 3-5 minutes. Many tasters use slightly lower temp (~80°C) and longer single steep for western-style cup.

notes:
Premium-grade Xihu Long Jing — one of China's top ten teas. Flat,
smooth, yellow-green leaves with a bean/chestnut aroma and clean
sweetness. Lighter and more delicate than the standard Dragon Well;
not as expensive as the Ming Qian Nonpareil grade but holds up well
for daily drinking. Spring harvest only; like all green teas, freshness
matters and flavor degrades within 6-12 months of harvest.

producer.name: Nagasaki I's Farm
producer.history: Small Nagasaki-based farm. Nagasaki is one of Japan's two main loquat (biwa) production regions alongside Chiba; the smaller 'Mogi' loquat variety is native to Nagasaki City. Biwa-cha is made from the leaves (not the fruit) of the loquat tree, traditionally used in Japanese folk medicine and listed in the Pharmacopoeia of Japan since 1886. Caffeine-free.

brewing.notes: Decoction-style brewing typical of Japanese herbal tisanes: 5g leaves in 500ml of near-boiling water, simmer or steep 3-5 minutes. Liquor turns reddish-brown / honey-colored. Can be re-steeped for a lighter second cup. Drinkable hot or cold-brewed; cold brew works well as a summer beverage. Caffeine-free, suitable for evening or for those avoiding caffeine.

notes:
Not strictly tea (Camellia sinensis) — a tisane made from loquat
(Eriobotrya japonica) leaves. Earthy, slightly sweet, light fruity
finish. Traditionally consumed for its claimed digestive and
respiratory benefits, though Francesco can decide whether to keep
any health claims out of the public catalog. Very stable: dried
leaves don't degrade quickly. Good caffeine-free option.

name.primary: Lapsang Souchong (Boston Tea Party historical collection)

producer.name: Oliver Pluff & Co. (for Boston Tea Party Ships & Museum)
producer.history: Hand-packaged by Oliver Pluff & Co. (Charleston, South Carolina) for the Boston Tea Party Ships & Museum gift shop. Sold as part of the 'Teas of the Boston Tea Party Collection,' a 5-tube set (~9-15g each) representing the five teas thrown overboard during the 1773 Boston Tea Party: Bohea, Congou, Lapsang Souchong, Singlo, and Hyson (per the museum's historical research). The original 1773 cargo included 10 chests of Souchong (~684 lbs total). Souchong itself originates from the Wuyi Mountains of Fujian, China — the term derives from Hokkien 正山小种 zhèngshān xiǎozhǒng meaning 'small-leaf cultivar from the original mountain.' Modern souchong exports are typically pinewood-smoked (Lapsang Souchong); 18th-century souchong would have had only a faint smoky character from the drying process.

acquisition.location: Boston Tea Party Ships & Museum gift shop
acquisition.context: Red Hat Summit Boston work trip

brewing.notes: Western brewing: 1-2 tsp (2-3g) per 250ml boiling water, 3-5 minutes. Lapsang Souchong is a polarizing tea — its pinewood smoke aroma is reminiscent of campfire, bacon, or Islay whisky. Single infusion mostly; the smoke fades quickly on second steep. Pairs well with strong-flavored foods (smoked meats, blue cheese) and whisky. Adding milk is unconventional but works for a 'smoked breakfast tea' effect.

notes:
Smoky black tea from Fujian's Wuyi Mountains, sold here as a
historical commemorative item. The museum collection includes
this in a 5-tube set with the other teas thrown overboard in 1773.
Modern Lapsang is more aggressively smoked than its 18th-century
predecessor. Black teas keep extremely well — stable urgency.

Note: this is a souvenir-grade tea, not a premium artisanal Lapsang.
For comparison: Wuyi Star or Yunshen brands offer higher-grade
unsmoked or lightly-smoked Zhengshan Xiaozhong that emphasize
the original tea character (longan/dried fruit notes) rather
than the heavy modern smoke.

producer.name: Canada True (Heart Industries Ltd.)
producer.history: Canada True is a Canadian souvenir brand owned by Heart Industries Ltd., specializing in maple-flavored gift-shop products: tea, cookies, fudge, and pure maple syrup. Their tea range targets the Canadian souvenir market with maple-flavored variants on standard tea bases (black, green, herbal, blueberry, ice wine). The Maple Black Tea uses 'high-grown Ceylon' leaves with 'natural identical Canadian maple syrup flavour.'

brewing.notes: Per Canada True instructions: 1 tea bag per cup, boiling water, 3-5 minutes hot. For iced: brew 1 bag with 1/3 cup boiling water, steep 3-5 min, remove bag, top with cold water and ice. Single infusion (tea bag format).

notes:
Canadian souvenir tea — high-grown Ceylon black base with synthetic
maple flavoring (the label specifies 'natural identical' flavor,
i.e., a flavoring chemically identical to natural maple compounds
but not derived from actual maple syrup). Sweetish, mild, easy-drinking;
not a serious tasting tea.

Sold typically as 23g/10-bag boxes or 50g/25-bag tins or 60g/30-bag
tins. Shelf-stable. Pairs well with breakfast pastries and
Canadian-themed gifting contexts.

producer.name: Canada True (Heart Industries Ltd.)
producer.history: Canada True is a Canadian souvenir brand owned by Heart Industries Ltd., specializing in maple-flavored gift-shop products. The Maple Green Tea blends a generic green tea base with 'natural identical Canadian maple syrup flavour.' Sold typically alongside the maple black and maple herbal variants in 3-pack souvenir sets.

brewing.notes: Producer doesn't publish green-tea-specific instructions; standard green tea brewing applies: 1 tea bag in 200-250ml at 75-85°C, 2-3 minutes. Boiling water will turn the green tea bitter, so let the kettle cool 1-2 minutes after boil before pouring. Single infusion (tea bag format).

notes:
Canadian souvenir tea — green tea base with synthetic maple
flavoring. Specific cultivar/origin of the green tea base is not
disclosed. Mild, slightly sweet, easy-drinking; a novelty rather
than a tasting tea.

Green tea bases have shorter shelf life than black, but the maple
flavoring extends acceptable use somewhat. Sold typically as
23g/10-bag boxes in 3-pack souvenir sets (with maple black and
maple herbal). Drink within ~12 months for best flavor.

producer.name: Canada True (Heart Industries Ltd.)
producer.history: Canada True is a Canadian souvenir brand owned by Heart Industries Ltd. The Maple Herbal Tea is a caffeine-free herbal blend with maple flavoring. Per the Amazon allergen warning, the blend potentially contains: ginger, lemongrass, orange peel, cardamom, hibiscus, safflower, turmeric, honey, rose hip, lemon peel — though the exact ingredient list per box may vary. No actual Camellia sinensis tea leaves; classifies as a tisane.

brewing.notes: Standard herbal tisane brewing: 1 tea bag per 200-250ml, boiling water, 5-7 minutes (longer than black tea — herbs and dried flowers need extended time to release their volatiles). Caffeine-free, suitable for evening drinking. Single infusion.

notes:
Canadian souvenir tisane — caffeine-free herbal blend with maple
flavoring. The exact herb mix isn't specified on standard product
pages, but the allergen warning suggests it likely contains a mix
including hibiscus (for color and tartness), lemongrass and citrus
peels (brightness), ginger/cardamom (warmth), and honey/rose hip
(sweetness/body) — a relatively standard 'gift shop herbal' base
customized with maple flavoring.

Caffeine-free → suitable for evening or anytime use. Stable shelf
life thanks to dried herbs/flowers — no urgency.

name.primary: Canopy by Hilton Osaka Umeda — The Original Blend Tea

producer.name: Canopy by Hilton Osaka Umeda
producer.history: Hotel-exclusive blend from Canopy by Hilton Osaka Umeda, located in the Grand Green Osaka complex next to Umekita Park near Osaka Station. The blend is described on its enclosed card as 'inspired by the beautiful scenery of Umekita Park': a sophisticated mix of black tea (subtly infused with grape aroma), Japanese green tea, and violet petals. Hotel-branded souvenir; the actual tea blender is not credited.

brewing.notes: Western-style brewing (the natural fit for a flavored blend): ~3g per 250ml at 95°C, 3-5 minutes. Black tea base sets the temperature; the green tea component is added to the blend in proportion that doesn't require lower temp. Single infusion — flavored blends with petals and aromatics generally don't re-steep meaningfully.

notes:
Hotel souvenir blend. Sophisticated marketing premise — black tea
with grape aroma + Japanese green tea + violet petals, intended to
evoke the scenery of Umekita Park. Not a traditional category,
closer to a Western-style aromatic blend than a Japanese tea.
Stable shelf life: dried petals and aromatic blends keep well for
many months sealed. Probably best as an afternoon/casual tea or
conversation piece rather than a serious tasting tea.

producer.name: Çaykur
producer.history: State-backed Turkish tea producer founded in the Rize region, central to modern Turkish tea culture.

brewing.notes: Traditionally brewed strong in the upper kettle of a Turkish double teapot and diluted to taste with hot water.

notes:
One of the most iconic everyday Turkish black teas, produced from tea
grown around the humid Black Sea region of Rize. Strong, brisk and smooth,
designed for repeated daily consumption in small tulip-shaped glasses.
A foundational tea for traditional Turkish breakfast culture and social rituals.

photo.alt: Yellow package of Çaykur Rize Turist Çayı black tea

producer.name: Çaykur
producer.history: Major Turkish tea producer associated with the Black Sea tea-growing regions.

brewing.notes: Usually brewed concentrated and softened with additional hot water when serving.

notes:
Premium-style Turkish black tea sourced from the Tirebolu area, a region
known for high-quality Black Sea tea cultivation. Compared to standard
Rize blends, Tirebolu 42 is often considered softer, more aromatic and
slightly sweeter while still maintaining the classic Turkish tea intensity.
Popular among enthusiasts looking for a more refined everyday çay experience.

photo.alt: Package of Çaykur Tirebolu 42 Turkish black tea

producer.name: Çaykur
producer.history: Çay-Kur is the Turkish state-owned tea producer (full name Çay İşletmeleri Genel Müdürlüğü), founded in 1971 and headquartered in Rize, on the Black Sea coast. Turkey is one of the world's largest tea consumers per capita, and Çaykur is the dominant domestic producer. 'Tomurcuk' means 'bud' in Turkish — the Tomurcuk line is positioned as a higher-grade product using fresh tea buds. The Earl Grey variant blends Çaykur's Black Sea-grown black tea with natural bergamot essence.

brewing.notes: Per Çaykur instructions: 1 tsp per 8oz (240ml) boiling water, 3-5 minutes. Traditional Turkish preparation uses a double-pot (çaydanlık): brew concentrated tea in upper pot, dilute with hot water from lower pot to taste. For Western-style: brew at full concentration. The bergamot is gentler than UK Earl Greys (Twinings, Ahmad), making it a softer entry into the style.

notes:
Turkish-style Earl Grey: the base is Black Sea-grown Camellia sinensis
(a different terroir than Sri Lankan or Kenyan teas, with a more
vegetal, slightly tannic character), perfumed with natural bergamot.
Lighter and brighter than UK Earl Greys, less full-bodied. Black
teas are very shelf-stable; bergamot oil aroma fades over time but
the tea itself keeps fine.

Available formats: 125g, 200g, 500g loose-leaf tins; also as tea
bags (40 sachets / 200g). Çaykur claims pesticide-free cultivation.

producer.name: Chunyuan Tea Houses
producer.history: Chunyuan Tea Houses (椿源居) is a Chinese tea brand distributed primarily through Asian groceries in North America. Limited public information about the parent company; positioned as a value-tier brand for everyday Chinese tea drinkers. Range includes oolong, herbal blends (chrysanthemum, longan/jujube), and pu-erh products. Often sold pre-portioned in tea bags rather than loose-leaf.

brewing.notes: Western brewing: 1 tea bag (or 2-3g loose) per 200-250ml, boiling water, 3-5 minutes. The chrysanthemum cools/balances the earthy pu-erh and adds floral notes. For loose-leaf gongfu (if format allows): rinse the pu-erh leaves with boiling water for 5s and discard, then steep 100°C, 15-30s, 30s, 45s. Chrysanthemum tolerates re-steeping.

notes:
Classic Chinese herbal-tea pairing: pu-erh (typically shou/ripe
pu-erh, dark and earthy) softened with dried chrysanthemum flowers
(light, floral, slightly bitter-sweet). The chrysanthemum is
traditionally believed to 'cool' the body and 'clear the eyes'
in Chinese tea/medicine — paired with pu-erh to balance the dark
tea's warming nature. Pu-erh keeps well long-term; the dried
flowers also stable. Stable urgency.

producer.name: Chunyuan Tea Houses
producer.history: Chunyuan Tea Houses (椿源居) is a Chinese tea brand distributed mainly via Asian groceries in North America. Their organic oolong is sold in 100-tea-bag boxes (~168g total, ~1.7g per bag) — a value/everyday-tier product rather than a traditional gongfu single-origin oolong. Producer claims hand-picked organic leaves from 'tea gardens nestled in the heart of Asia,' but specific origin (Fujian, Taiwan, etc.) is not disclosed.

brewing.notes: Per producer instructions: 1 tea bag in 250ml boiling water, steep 3-4 minutes. Boiling water is appropriate for oolong but in this product the leaf grade and roasting level are unspecified, so brewing is approximated as a moderately oxidized 'standard' oolong. A second steep is feasible but will be lighter than the first. Don't compare to gongfu-style preparations of premium oolongs (Tie Guan Yin, Da Hong Pao, etc.) — this is a different category.

notes:
Generic commercial oolong in tea-bag format. Without disclosed
cultivar, oxidation level, or roast profile, expect a middle-of-
the-road semi-oxidized green/amber liquor with light floral and
honey notes. Stable shelf life; oolong is more forgiving than
green tea on storage. Best treated as an everyday/utility tea
rather than a tasting tea.

brewing.notes: Full boiling water recommended for herbal blends.

notes:
Austrian herbal infusion blend ("forest herb mix") containing assorted
herbs, flowers and berries. Naturally caffeine-free and intended for
relaxing evening consumption. The packaging has the reassuring aesthetic
of a wise alpine grandmother who definitely knows which mushroom not to eat.

photo.alt: Paper bag of Demmers Teehaus Waldkräutermischung herbal tea

name.alternate: 西湖龙井 (Xī Hú Lóng Jǐng)

producer.name: Golden Bridge brand
producer.history: Generic Chinese export brand; specific producing entity not well-documented in English-language sources. Multiple Chinese tea exporters use 'Golden Bridge' or '金桥' branding for value-tier teas distributed via Asian grocery channels. The product is a 'West Lake Longjing' (Xī Hú Lóng Jǐng), one of China's Ten Famous Teas, traditionally produced around West Lake in Hangzhou, Zhejiang province. Authentic core-area Longjing requires anti-counterfeiting certification per the Hangzhou West Lake Longjing Tea Industry Association (since 2020) — an unbranded export tea labelled 'West Lake Longjing' may or may not actually originate from the protected core area.

brewing.notes: Western/Chinese-style: 3g per 150ml at 80-85°C, 2-3 minutes (or 'wait 10 minutes' per some packaging). Use a tall glass to watch leaves dance and unfurl — Longjing is traditionally appreciated visually. Gongfu schedule alternative: 4g/100ml in gaiwan, 80°C, 30s/45s/60s. Chestnut/bean notes; should not be bitter. If bitter, lower the temperature.

notes:
Likely a value-tier Longjing of unverified core-area origin.
Standard pan-fired Chinese green tea: flat blade-shaped leaves,
pale jade liquor, chestnut/bean fragrance. Premium Longjing
(Mingqian or pre-Qingming) commands prices of $200+/kg from
certified core-area producers; value-tier export brands typically
source from outer West Lake areas or Yuezhou/Qiantang regions.
Green teas have short shelf life (12-18 months max); urgency 'now'
unless acquisition was very recent.

producer.name: Ikeda Senchado
producer.history: Private brand of Ikeda Seicha Co. (founded 1948), Tenmonkan, Kagoshima. Run by tea master Kenta Ikeda (茶師十段). This premium hōjicha is made by roasting gyokuro leaves (rather than bancha or stems) over the shop's signature 'lit-flame' (烈火) high-heat roasting process. The roasting preserves residual umami from the shaded gyokuro base while developing nutty, toasted notes.

brewing.notes: Premium hōjicha brewing — slightly lower temp than standard hōjicha to preserve residual umami from the gyokuro base. First infusion: 90°C, 30s. Second: 95°C, 15s. Standard hōjicha can take full-boil water but this gyokuro-base version benefits from gentler treatment.

notes:
Luxury hōjicha made from roasted gyokuro leaves, not bancha. Carries
a deeper sweetness and residual umami that regular hōjicha lacks.
The roasted character makes it more stable than other Kagoshima teas
in this collection — degrades slowly, no rush to open. A natural
end-of-day or after-dinner tea (low caffeine, comforting, warming).
Note: distinct from Kanbayashi's Hojicha-from-Gyokuro (Uji-based)
in the existing collection — Ikeda's version uses Kagoshima base
and a more aggressive roast.

producer.name: Mitsuyasu Seikaen
producer.history: Historic Fukuoka tea shop founded in 1716, currently in its 13th generation. Based in Hakata (Fukuoka City), specializes in Yame tea. Yame (Fukuoka prefecture) is one of Japan's premier gyokuro production regions and accounts for approximately half of Japan's gyokuro output. The shop runs an adjoining tea house (Chayu Seika) and supplies traditional tea ceremony practitioners.

brewing.notes: Yame-style gyokuro brewing: very low temperature, high leaf-to-water ratio. First infusion: 50°C, 120s. Second: 50°C, 30s. Third: 60°C, 60s. Cool the water (boil and let cool, or pour through cooling vessels). Use shiboridashi or houhin for low-temperature pours; kyusu also works. Yame gyokuro often takes a slightly higher temperature than Uji gyokuro.

notes:
Authentic Yame gyokuro from a 300+ year old tea shop. Yame is regarded
as Japan's top gyokuro region alongside Uji; its gyokuro tends to be
full-bodied with strong umami. Shaded for ~20 days before harvest to
develop the characteristic L-theanine sweetness. Ideal companion: a
small piece of wagashi to balance the intense umami.

producer.name: Kanbayashi Shunsho Honten
producer.history: One of Japan's oldest tea houses, founded in the Eiroku era (1558-1569), now in its 14th generation. Suppliers to the Tokugawa Shogunate — the Kanbayashi family held the highest rank of 'Goyō ochashi' (御物御茶師, official imperial tea masters) under the early Tokugawa shogunate. Centuries-old lineage from Uji. The family contributed to the development of the Kyō Midori (gyokuro) and Uji Hikari (tencha/gyokuro) cultivars used today. Operates from Uji-bashi Dori in Uji City.

brewing.notes: Premium Uji gyokuro — extreme low-temp, high leaf-density brewing. First infusion: 50°C, 120s. Second: 50°C, 30s. Third: 60°C, 60s. Cool the water carefully (boil, pour through cooling vessels). Use shiboridashi or houhin; they're designed for these slow low-temp pours. The first brew should be syrup-thick with nori-like umami; subsequent rounds get progressively more vegetal. Acquired Sep 2025 — already 6+ months old at time of cataloging, gyokuro loses umami fast.

notes:
Pinnacle Uji gyokuro from one of Japan's most historically significant
tea houses (Tokugawa-era imperial suppliers). Honeymoon-acquisition
piece — kept for special occasions, but gyokuro doesn't wait. Per
consumption priority, this should be opened and finished first among
all the Uji-collection teas.

producer.name: Harrods
producer.history: Harrods is a luxury department store in Knightsbridge, London, founded in 1849. No. 14 English Breakfast is described by Harrods as their 'best-selling' tea: a 4-region blend of full-bodied Assam, crisp Ceylon, floral Darjeeling, and bright Kenyan, formulated to hold its strength when served with milk. Available in both loose-leaf (125g, 200g caddies) and tea-bag formats.

brewing.notes: Western brewing: 1 tsp (~3g) per 250ml boiling water, 3-5 minutes. Designed to take milk well — add a splash after brewing. Slightly higher leaf-to-water ratio than afternoon teas because the blend is built for strength. Single infusion.

notes:
Classic 4-region English Breakfast blend. Punchy, full-bodied,
with a malty-bright character that holds up to milk and sugar.
Designed as a morning tea, traditionally served with a full
English breakfast or as a wake-up brew. Higher caffeine than
the Afternoon Ceylon (No. 16) due to the Assam component.

Black tea — stable shelf life. Available format: 125g, 200g loose-leaf
caddies, plus 50-bag and 100-bag tin formats.

producer.name: Harrods
producer.history: Harrods is a luxury department store in Knightsbridge, London, founded in 1849 by Charles Henry Harrod. The Harrods tea range is one of its iconic food-hall offerings, with each blend assigned a numbered identifier. No. 16 Afternoon Ceylon is positioned as a single-origin afternoon tea: 'a single origin tea from the pristine mountains of Sri Lanka,' marketed as light and refreshing for daytime drinking.

brewing.notes: Western brewing: 1 tsp per 250ml boiling water, 3-5 minutes. Per Harrods: also excellent iced — brew per instructions, add honey to taste, cool to room temperature, pour over ice. Single-origin Ceylon doesn't take milk as well as breakfast blends; it's best appreciated black with a slice of lemon.

notes:
Single-origin highland Ceylon, positioned as an 'afternoon' tea
(lighter and brighter than morning breakfast blends). Sweet amber
liquor with citrus tints; characteristic Ceylon brisk-but-not-tannic
profile. Marketed for use with afternoon-tea service.

Available format: 125g loose-leaf tin caddy (and likely 50-bag
tea-bag tin). Black tea — stable shelf life.

producer.name: Harrods
producer.history: Harrods is a luxury department store in Knightsbridge, London, founded in 1849. No. 42 Earl Grey is a high-bergamot Earl Grey blending East African (predominantly Kenyan) and Indian black teas. Per Harrods: 'high proportion of quality Kenyan leaves to give the brew its lively, brisk flavour' complemented with 'exotic citrus from our exclusive bergamot flavouring.'

brewing.notes: Western brewing: 1 tsp per 250ml boiling water, 3-5 minutes. Earl Grey is traditionally taken black or with lemon (not milk) — milk dampens the bergamot character. Single infusion.

notes:
High-bergamot Earl Grey on a Kenyan-dominant base. Brighter and
livelier than UK-classic blends that lean on Ceylon or Indian
bases — Kenyan tea provides a notably brisk, almost citrusy
black-tea backbone that complements the bergamot oil.

Available formats: 125g, 200g loose-leaf caddies, plus 50-bag
and 100-bag tea-bag tins. Black tea — stable shelf life;
bergamot oil aroma fades over months but the tea itself holds
up indefinitely.

producer.name: Nakamura Tokichi Honten
producer.history: Established in Uji in 1854. The main store buildings are designated as Important Cultural Landscapes by the Japanese government (Meiji-era tea merchant architecture). The Nakamura family contributed to developing the Kyōmidori (gyokuro) and Ujihikari (tencha/gyokuro) cultivars at the Kyoto Prefectural Tea Research Institute. Hatsu no Mukashi (初の昔, 'First of Old') is the top-tier in their 'Mukashi' line of poetic-named matchas; aged 1-2 years before sale to develop full umami. Curated in collaboration with the Urasenke Grand Tea Master.

brewing.notes: Designed primarily for koicha (thick tea): 4g matcha + 30ml water at 80°C, kneaded slowly with chasen until smooth and syrupy (no foam). Also drinkable as usucha (thin tea): 2g + 70ml at 80°C, whisked to a fine froth. Sift before whisking to avoid lumps. Matcha at 6+ months from canning is past peak — consume promptly. Once opened, finish in 2-3 weeks; refrigerate sealed in original tin.

notes:
Top-tier ceremonial matcha from a 170+ year-old Uji tea house.
Velvety mouthfeel, deep umami sweetness, almost no astringency,
vibrant jade color — hallmarks of a premium koicha-grade matcha.
Acquired Sep 2025 honeymoon — the powder oxidizes faster than other
forms of tea, so this is one of the highest-priority items in the
collection to consume.

brewing.notes: Japanese black teas are often lighter and smoother than Indian or Ceylon styles.

notes:
Loose-leaf Japanese black tea stored in a labelled plastic bag. Specific
cultivar and producer unknown. Likely a wakoucha-style tea with softer,
sweeter and less tannic characteristics than typical Western black teas.
Modest bag, potentially excellent tea — the tea equivalent of a jazz musician in an unmarked basement bar.

photo.alt: Loose-leaf Japanese black tea in a plastic storage bag

producer.name: TeaVivre
producer.history: Online Chinese tea retailer founded in 2011, sourcing directly from tea gardens. This blend uses high-mountain Jinggu Dabai Cha leaves from the Wen Shan tea garden in Jinggu County, Yunnan (~1800m altitude), hand-rolled into pearls and scented with jasmine flowers from Hengxian, Guangxi (China's primary jasmine production region). Three rounds of scenting plus a final 'tihua' top-scenting (三窨一提).

brewing.notes: Gongfu schedule (110ml gaiwan, 4g): rinse, 40s, 40s, 40s, 40s, 50s, 60s, 80s, 100s, 120s, 150s. Western: 3g per 250ml at 85°C, 3-5 minutes.

notes:
Yunnan-base jasmine pearls. Sweeter and more delicate than Fujian-base
versions, with an intense jasmine fragrance and lingering aftertaste.
Brews many infusions before fading. Distinguishable from the Long Zhu
variant (Fujian-base) by the deeper, fuller tea body underneath the
jasmine layer.

name.primary: Jasmine Dragon Pearls Long Zhu Green Tea

producer.name: TeaVivre
producer.history: Online Chinese tea retailer founded in 2011. Long Zhu pearls produced at the Wengxi Tea Garden in Fuding, Fujian. Tea base picked one bud + one or two leaves; hand-rolled into pearls. Crafted by Mr. Chen, a tea master with 40+ years of jasmine tea experience. Five rounds of scenting plus a final 'yi-ti' top-scenting using first-grade jasmine flowers (6-8 hours, 6-8kg flowers per 100kg of tea).

brewing.notes: Gongfu schedule (110ml gaiwan, 5g): rinse, 40s, 50s, 60s, 80s, 100s, 120s. Western: 5g per 355ml at 85°C, 5-8 minutes.

notes:
Fujian-base jasmine pearls, distinct from the Yunnan version. Refreshing,
soft and sweet; resists bitterness even when brewed at high temperature
or for long durations. The "Long Zhu" in the name is literally
"Dragon Pearls" — the redundancy is part of the SKU naming convention.
Less of a deep-mountain character than the Yunnan version; more polished
jasmine intensity due to the extra 'yi-ti' final scenting step.

producer.name: Marukyu Koyamaen
producer.history: Historic Uji-based matcha producer. Kiun is part of the Tea Schools Favored Matcha line, selected by Hōunsai (Sen Genshitsu, the 15th Grand Tea Master of Urasenke). Marukyu Koyamaen consistently wins top awards at the National Tea Competition. Note: this tea is produced in Uji, not in Kyushu — it was acquired during a Kyushu trip (likely from a tea shop reselling Marukyu line) but the production origin is Uji.

brewing.notes: Primarily a koicha (thick tea): 4g matcha + 30ml water at 80°C, kneaded slowly with chasen. Also drinkable as usucha (thin tea): 2g matcha + 70ml water at 80°C, whisked to a fine froth. Sift before whisking. Best-before period from Marukyu: 7 months from canning (refrigerate after opening, finish in 2-3 weeks).

notes:
Ceremonial-grade matcha favored by the 15th Grand Tea Master of
Urasenke. Pumpkin-seed-like aroma, full-bodied umami sweetness with
almost no astringency. Designed for koicha but works exceptionally
well as everyday usucha; widely regarded as one of the best
value-for-money premium matchas.

acquisition.context: Purchased from an Italian tea shop

brewing.notes: Kukicha is made from stems and twigs, so it brews lighter and sweeter than leaf-based teas. Lower caffeine than sencha.

notes:
Kukicha (twig tea) sourced from an Italian tea shop — exact producer
and origin unknown. Made from stems, stalks and twigs of the tea plant,
giving it a naturally sweet, nutty and mild character with low caffeine.
A gentle everyday tea, popular in macrobiotic diets.

brewing.notes: Longer steeps will emphasize the floral rose character.

notes:
Aromatic flavoured black tea blending lychee sweetness, raspberry fruit
character and floral rose notes. Likely designed as a fragrant dessert-style
tea with a smooth black tea base.

photo.alt: Black tin of flavoured black tea with lychee raspberry and rose flavour

brewing.notes: Avoid boiling water to preserve the fruit aromas and prevent bitterness.

notes:
Flavoured green tea blend combining pineapple, lime and ginger notes.
Intended as a bright, tropical everyday tea with a refreshing citrus
profile and mild spice finish.

photo.alt: Black tin of flavoured green tea with pineapple lemon ginger flavours

producer.name: Ikeda Senchado
producer.history: Private brand of Ikeda Seicha Co. (founded 1948), based in Tenmonkan, Kagoshima. Run by tea master Kenta Ikeda — 茶師十段 (10th-dan tea master), a rare top-level certification. Distinctive 'lit-flame' (烈火) roasting and blending style. Kagoshima-grown matcha is rare; Ikeda Senchado is one of the few producers offering certified-organic Kagoshima matcha. Specializes in showcasing Kagoshima's tea character (mellow southern-plain teas vs. aromatic northern-mountain teas).

brewing.notes: Usucha-style: 2g matcha + 70ml water at 80°C, whisked with chasen in a zigzag motion until a fine froth forms. Sift before whisking. For koicha: 4g + 30ml at 80°C, kneaded slowly. Refrigerate after opening, finish within 2-3 weeks for optimal freshness.

notes:
Single-origin Kagoshima matcha — fresher and sweeter than typical
Uji matchas, with the distinctive character of Kyushu southern teas.
Kagoshima sees earlier spring harvests than Uji (warmer climate),
which can give a slightly different umami profile. A great
counterpoint to Uji-centric matcha collections.

producer.name: Unknown (Ujitawara)
producer.history: Producer: small Ujitawara producer (specific name not recorded — Ujitawara is a small town in Tsuzuki District, Kyoto, considered the historical birthplace of Japanese sencha and an important matcha-growing area). 'Kuwari' is a labeling/grade term used by the producer. The defining feature: the matcha is single-cultivar Samidori (さみどり), a classic Uji cultivar prized for koicha — vivid green color, high amino-acid content, smooth umami. Most matchas are blended; single-cultivar matcha is a connoisseur's choice.

brewing.notes: Single-cultivar Samidori — try both usucha (2g + 70ml at 80°C, whisked) and koicha (4g + 30ml at 80°C, kneaded) to taste the cultivar's character without blending. Samidori is traditionally a koicha-leaning cultivar but works in both. Sift before whisking. Same freshness caveats as other honeymoon-acquired matchas: 6+ months from canning, consume promptly.

notes:
Single-cultivar Samidori matcha — a connoisseur piece. Most matchas
are intentional blends to balance umami, sweetness, and astringency;
single-cultivar versions let you taste one cultivar's specific
character, which is rare and didactic for a matcha enthusiast.
Samidori is one of the classic Uji cultivars, paired with the more
modern Goko and Asahi for premium tencha. The 'Kuwari' designation
on the producer's labeling is not a widely-catalogued term — Francesco
may have additional context from purchase.

brewing.notes: Designed primarily for lattes, smoothies and baking rather than traditional usucha preparation.

notes:
Unsweetened Japanese culinary-grade matcha powder marketed for baking,
smoothies and lattes. Likely blended for versatility and stronger flavor
retention in milk-based or sweet preparations rather than ceremonial
drinking. The packaging screams 'I can become ice cream at any moment.'

photo.alt: Container of Matcha Love Japanese Culinary Matcha

brewing.notes: Potential entry-level matcha. Lower temperatures help reduce bitterness.

notes:
Tin-labelled matcha tea with Chinese/Japanese branding elements. Exact
origin and grade unclear from the packaging alone. Likely intended for
everyday consumption or culinary use rather than formal tea ceremony.
The tin, however, has excellent 'mysterious tea shop found during a rainy side quest' energy.

photo.alt: Green tin of Matcha Tea with Asian characters

producer.name: Mighty Leaf
producer.history: Mighty Leaf is a US tea brand founded in 1996 in Mill Valley, California; acquired in 2014 by Peet's Coffee (now part of JDE Peet's). Their organic matcha is sourced from Kagoshima and Shizuoka prefectures in Japan, stone-milled in temperature-controlled, sealed production rooms to preserve freshness. The leaves are tencha (shade-grown for ~3 weeks before harvest, de-veined, and powdered). Marketed as ceremonial grade — bright emerald, creamy, with bittersweet floral notes. Standard fine usucha-grade powder rather than a koicha-tier specialty.

brewing.notes: Usucha-style preparation: 1 tsp (~2g) sifted matcha + 60-70ml hot water (~80°C, just below boiling), whisk briskly with a chasen in W or M motion until light froth forms (~20-30 seconds). Mighty Leaf packaging suggests 1 tsp + 8oz hot water — that's a Western 'matcha tea' style, more dilute than traditional usucha. For a latte: same matcha with steamed milk, optional sweetener.

notes:
US-blended ceremonial-grade matcha sourced from two of Japan's
major tea regions. Quality is decent for a US grocery channel
brand but won't match Uji single-cultivar matchas (Marukyu Koyamaen,
Ippodo, Nakamura Tokichi). Suitable for usucha, lattes, and culinary
use; not ideal for koicha (thick tea) which needs higher grades.

Matcha has VERY short shelf life once opened — finish within 2-3
weeks for best flavor and color. Refrigerate sealed; keep away
from light, heat, and moisture. Urgency 'now' applies as soon as
the tin is opened.

producer.name: Nakamura Tokichi Honten
producer.history: Established in Uji in 1854. Main store designated as Important Cultural Landscape. 'Miyako no Tatsumi' (都の巽, 'Southeast of the Capital') refers to Uji's geographic position relative to old Kyoto (the imperial capital). Uji-style sencha is more delicate and umami-forward than typical Shizuoka-style senchas — closer in character to gyokuro than to fukamushi-style senchas, often with slight shading.

brewing.notes: Uji-style sencha is delicate — keep water cool to preserve umami. First infusion: 70°C, 90s. Second: 75°C, 15s. Third: 80°C, 45s. If the leaves look slightly shaded (kabusecha-influenced), drop the first temp to 65°C. 6+ months from purchase — sencha holds up better than matcha but is still declining; consume in the next few weeks.

notes:
Uji-style sencha from Nakamura Tokichi — delicate, umami-forward,
kabusecha-adjacent character (likely with some shading before
harvest). Stylistically closer to gyokuro than to standard sencha
in temperament. Best brewed gently to avoid extracting bitterness.
Honeymoon-purchase, 6+ months old at cataloging — flavor still
good but declining; finish in coming weeks.

name.primary: Nonpareil Te Gong Huang Shan Mao Feng Green Tea

producer.name: TeaVivre
producer.history: Pre-Qing Ming spring harvest from high-altitude tea trees on Mt. Huangshan in Shexian County, Anhui Province. Made by Mr. Ke at Dailing Tea Garden, a tea master with 40+ years of experience specializing in traditional Mao Feng craftsmanship. 'Te Gong' (特贡) marks the highest grade — historically used as imperial tribute. Sparrow-tongue shaped buds from Huangshan large-leaf cultivar.

brewing.notes: APPROXIMATED from standard Huang Shan Mao Feng parameters — TeaVivre does not publish gongfu schedule for the Te Gong nonpareil grade. Gongfu schedule (110ml gaiwan, 4g): rinse, 30s, 45s, 70s, 100s. Western: 2g per 250ml at 85°C, 3-5 minutes. Pre-ming green teas are delicate; consider lower temp (~80°C) and shorter first steep if bitterness develops.

notes:
Pre-Qing Ming (early spring, before April 5) harvest, considered the
most precious window for green tea. Bird-tongue (sparrow-tongue) shape:
the leaves stand vertically when brewed in glass, then slowly sink.
Smooth and sweet with orchid-like floral aroma, hints of chestnut and
toasted rice. One of China's top ten green teas. Best consumed within
6-12 months of harvest while the freshness peaks.

name.primary: The Republic of Tea Organic Ceremonial Matcha

producer.name: The Republic of Tea
producer.history: The Republic of Tea is a US tea brand founded in 1992 in Northern California by Mel and Patricia Ziegler (Banana Republic founders) and Bill Rosenzweig. Their Organic Ceremonial Matcha is described as 'organic shade-grown tea made from young tencha leaves and stone-ground into a fine powder' — Japan-sourced tencha, US-distributed. Sold in small tins (4 servings each) and as part of multi-matcha gift sets including flavored variants (Sweet Vanilla, Dubai Chocolate, Strawberry, Lavender).

brewing.notes: Per producer: ½-1 tsp matcha + 6oz (180ml) of just-short-of-boiling water (~175°F / 80°C); whisk for 1-2 minutes until well blended. NOTE: producer says 'just below boiling' which is somewhat vague — at >85°C matcha turns bitter, so aim for 75-80°C, not actually approaching 100°C. For traditional usucha use closer to 70°C and a thicker concentration (2g + 60ml). Iced/latte: blend ½-1 tsp with 8oz cold water or milk.

notes:
US-distributed ceremonial-grade matcha made from Japanese tencha
leaves. Stone-ground; smooth, mellow, vegetal sweetness, deep
green color. Comparable in tier to Mighty Leaf's organic matcha
— competent grocery-channel ceremonial-grade, suitable for usucha
and lattes but not for koicha or formal tea ceremony.

Matcha shelf life: short. Finish within 2-3 weeks once opened;
refrigerate sealed. Urgency 'now' applies as soon as the tin is
opened. Republic of Tea also sells flavored matcha variants under
the same brand line — the unflavored 'Organic Ceremonial' is the
cleanest and most versatile.

producer.name: Kusano Seichaen
producer.history: Tea producer and shop in Ureshino Onsen, Saga prefecture (Kyushu). 'Satsuki Tsuyu' (さつき露, 'May Dew') is one of their signature tamaryokucha products, marketed under the regional moniker 'Yushuku Meicha' (湯宿銘茶, 'famous tea of the hot-spring inn town' — Yushuku being a poetic reference to Ureshino's onsen-town identity). Kusano's 'Tsuyukabuse' tea has won the Fine Product Award at the National Japanese Tea Competition. Ureshino is one of the few Japanese regions still producing tamaryokucha at scale — a curled-leaf style predating the modern needle-shaped sencha.

brewing.notes: Tamaryokucha schedule: 75°C, 60s first; 80°C, 15s second; 80°C, 45s third. Tamaryokucha tolerates slightly hotter water than premium sencha — the curled leaves open more slowly. Tends to give a richer, rounder profile than needle-rolled sencha; less astringent.

notes:
Ureshino-style tamaryokucha — leaves curled into comma shapes (no
final rolling step), giving a softer, rounder, less astringent
profile than standard sencha. Buttery, marine, vegetal notes are
common. Tamaryokucha is more forgiving and shelf-stable than
premium sencha, which is why it falls in the 'calma' urgency tier
even though it's a green tea.

Note: this entry replaces the previous 'yushuku-meicha-kusano-seichaen'
file. After photo inspection, the SKU name on the packaging is
'Satsuki Tsuyu' and 'Yushuku Meicha' is the regional brand label,
not the product name itself.

name.primary: Tangerine Peel (Chen Pi) Liu Bao Cha Dark Tea

producer.name: TeaVivre
producer.history: Blend of 2013 Liu Bao tea (Wuzhou, Guangxi) with aged Xinhui mandarin peel (chenpi) from Guangdong. Liu Bao is a fermented dark tea named after Liu Bao Xiang in Guangxi, with pile-fermentation processing similar to ripe pu-erh. Xinhui is the most famous origin for chenpi — Citrus reticulata Blanco cv. Chachiensis — valued in Traditional Chinese Medicine for digestive properties. The combination is a classic post-meal tea in southern China.

brewing.notes: APPROXIMATED from TeaVivre's Bing Lang Xiang Liu Bao Cha 2014 (closest match — same producer, same category, same vintage range). Gongfu schedule (110ml gaiwan, 8g): rinse, 5s, 5s, 10s, 10s, 20s, 20s, 30s, 40s, 50s, 70s, 90s, 120s. Western: 4g per 355ml at 100°C, 5-8 minutes. Always rinse first to wake the leaf; use full-boil water for proper extraction.

notes:
Aged dark tea (heicha) blended with chenpi. Years of fermentation
give the Liu Bao base a deep earthy/woody profile; the aged Xinhui
peel adds a bright tangy citrus layer that balances the earthiness
with a sweet aftertaste. Mellower than ripe pu-erh — gentler on the
stomach. Improves with further aging; no urgency to consume quickly.
Excellent autumn/winter tea and traditional digestive after heavy meals.

producer.name: Jinjiang Yuan
producer.history: Chinese tea brand (晋江源茶业, 'Jinjiang Source Tea Company'), based in or near Jinjiang/Anxi, Fujian Province. Anxi County in southern Fujian is the historic birthplace of Tieguanyin and was named the 'Home of Chinese Oolong Tea' by the Ministry of Agriculture in 1995. Producer details limited — Francesco can confirm packaging notes if available. The Tieguanyin cultivar is grown across Anxi (Inner Anxi at 800-1000m+ for premium grades; Outer Anxi at lower altitudes for everyday grades).

brewing.notes: Gongfu schedule for qingxiang-style (lightly oxidized 'green' Tieguanyin): 95°C, flash steeps. Rinse first. First infusion: 95°C, 15s. Then 25s, 35s, 45s, 55s, 75s, 85s. For traditional roasted (nongxiang) style, use 100°C and similar timing. The packaging style determines: tightly rolled jade-green pearls = qingxiang; darker, twisted leaf = nongxiang.

notes:
Anxi Tieguanyin acquired in Japan (likely from a Chinese tea retailer
or Japanese tea shop with Chinese selection). Style (qingxiang/light
vs. nongxiang/roasted) not specified — Francesco's note 'gongfu 95°C
flash' suggests qingxiang style. Oolongs are semi-oxidized and
generally stable; qingxiang versions degrade faster than roasted
versions but still keep well for many months when sealed.

producer.name: Nakamura Tokichi Honten
producer.history: Established in Uji in 1854. Main store designated as Important Cultural Landscape (Meiji-era tea merchant architecture). 'Uji no Mukashi' (宇治の昔, 'Old Days of Uji') sits one tier below Hatsu no Mukashi in the Mukashi line, but is still a top-shelf ceremonial matcha. Like all tencha-based matchas from Nakamura Tokichi, leaves are stone-milled at slow speed to avoid heat-induced flavor loss.

brewing.notes: Versatile matcha — works well both as usucha and koicha. Usucha: 2g + 70ml water at 80°C, whisked. Koicha: 4g + 30ml at 80°C, kneaded. Sift before whisking. Same caveats as Hatsu no Mukashi: 6+ months from canning, finish in 2-3 weeks once opened, refrigerate sealed.

notes:
Ceremonial matcha from Nakamura Tokichi, one tier below Hatsu no
Mukashi but still excellent. Slightly more accessible price point
while maintaining the umami-forward, low-bitterness Nakamura
signature. Use this for daily ceremonial-style preparation while
saving Hatsu no Mukashi for special occasions — but consume both
promptly given honeymoon-acquisition timing.

producer.name: Unknown
producer.history: Producer not identified from packaging. Sold as a generic 'Uji matcha' SKU — typical of small Uji tea shops that distribute everyday-grade matcha in plain canisters without prominent branding. Uji (Kyoto prefecture) has been Japan's most prestigious matcha-producing region for centuries, but not every tea sold there is premium ceremonial grade — many shops offer affordable everyday matcha for daily use, lattes, or culinary purposes.

brewing.notes: Daily-use matcha — works well for usucha (2g + 70ml at 80°C, whisked) but its character is more straightforward than premium ceremonial grades; fine for matcha lattes, daily preparation, or as the user note suggests, even Keurig-style brewing. Sift before whisking. Once opened, finish in 2-3 weeks; refrigerate sealed.

notes:
Anonymous everyday-grade Uji matcha. Per Francesco's own consumption
plan: 'if giving away, do it soon; if keeping, use as daily (Keurig).'
This positions it as an everyday/utility matcha rather than a special
occasion piece — perfect for warming up with a quick bowl during the
workday or for matcha latte preparation. The 6+ months from honeymoon
acquisition mean the powder is past peak; consume promptly.

producer.name: Vahdam India
producer.history: Vahdam India is a Delhi-based direct-to-consumer tea brand founded in 2015 by Bala Sarda. They source from over 150 plantations across India's main tea regions (Darjeeling, Assam, Nilgiri, Kangra, Sikkim) and ship globally. Certified carbon-neutral and plastic-neutral, they donate 1% of revenue to tea-grower children's education through their 'Teach Me' initiative. Mint Melody is one of their flagship green-tea blends: high-grown Himalayan green tea (50%) + spearmint (25%) + peppermint (25%).

brewing.notes: Per Vahdam: 1 tea bag (or 2g loose) per 200ml, 3-5 minutes. The mint leaves are tolerant of slightly hotter water than pure green tea, so 80-90°C works (vs. the 70-75°C ideal for premium pure green tea). Can be enjoyed hot or iced; especially refreshing as a cold brew (4-6 hours in fridge with cold water, 1 bag/200ml). Single infusion in tea-bag format; loose-leaf tolerates a second steep.

notes:
Indian high-grown green tea blended with both spearmint (sweeter,
rounded) and peppermint (sharper, more cooling). The 50/50 mint
ratio is generous — this is firmly a mint-forward blend, not just
a 'hint of mint.' Pleasant cold-brewed; works as an iced tea base.

Available formats: loose-leaf (3.53oz / 100g resealable pouch),
pyramid tea bags (15ct or 30ct or 100ct, plant-based or nylon).
Vahdam claims 3-year shelf life from harvest date. Mint volatiles
fade over months — drink within ~12 months for best aroma.

producer.name: TeaVivre
producer.history: Sourced from the Nanguang Tea Garden west of Mt. Taimu in Panxi, Fuding, Fujian (600-900m elevation, 80% forested). Made from Fuding Dabaihao cultivar (national tea plant variety since 1985). Lightly oxidized white tea, processed via natural withering — same technique as Silver Needle, but using one bud + opened leaves rather than buds only. White Peony was first produced in the Shuiji area of Zhenghe county, Fujian, in the 1870s.

brewing.notes: Gongfu schedule (110ml gaiwan, 5g): rinse, 25s, 45s, 70s, 90s, 150s. Western: 2g per 355ml at 85°C, 5-8 minutes. Some sources recommend slightly hotter water (90°C) for entry-level Bai Mu Dan; experiment per palate.

notes:
Entry-level white tea — a balance between the fresh delicacy of Silver
Needle and the fuller character of older white tea grades. Apricot-yellow
liquor when brewed, with a soft, mellow profile, light floral notes,
and the characteristic pekoe/hay scent of Fuding white teas. White tea
ages well: aged Bai Mu Dan (3+ years) develops deeper, sweeter, more
complex notes — like aged pu-erh, value increases with proper storage.

producer.name: Yamamotoyama
producer.history: Japanese tea house founded in 1690 in Nihonbashi, Edo (modern Tokyo) by Kahei Yamamoto. One of Japan's oldest and most established tea companies. Their genmaicha combines steamed green tea leaves with roasted brown rice (some grains pop into puffy white kernels during roasting), historically a poor-person's tea that has become a beloved everyday Japanese tea worldwide.

origin.locality: Domestic Japanese tea leaves + roasted rice

brewing.notes: Per Yamamotoyama: 3-5g per 200-240ml at 80-90°C, 30s-1min. Genmaicha is more forgiving on temperature than premium sencha; the roasted rice tolerates and benefits from heat. Second steep retains the rice toastiness even as the green tea fades. Loose-leaf gives a much better extraction than tea bags.

notes:
Genmaicha = green tea + roasted rice. Toasty, comforting, vaguely
popcorn-like aroma; lower caffeine than straight sencha because
the rice is non-caffeinated and dilutes the leaf ratio. Yamamotoyama's
version is a competent everyday genmaicha — not a specialty
preparation, but reliable and widely available. Pairs well with
Japanese rice dishes, tempura, and savory snacks.

producer.name: Yamamotoyama
producer.history: Japanese tea house founded in 1690 in Nihonbashi, Edo (modern Tokyo) by Kahei Yamamoto. One of Japan's oldest and most established tea companies (335+ years), now operating as Yamamotoyama USA in addition to its Japanese operations. Their hojicha blends two roasting intensities: lightly roasted leaves for a bright top note and deeply roasted leaves for nutty depth. Made from 100% domestically grown Japanese tea leaves.

brewing.notes: Hojicha tolerates higher water temperatures than most green teas because the roasting has driven off astringent compounds. Per Yamamotoyama's instructions: 3-5g per 200-240ml at 80-90°C, 30s-1min. Second infusion will be lighter but still aromatic. Loose-leaf preferred over teabags; with bags use 30s-1min boiling water. Excellent cold-brewed (4+ hours in fridge).

notes:
Roasted (hojicha) green tea — lower caffeine than steamed green
teas thanks to the roasting process. Aroma profile: toasty,
caramelized, nutty, slightly woody. Easy on the stomach, suitable
for evening drinking and after meals. Yamamotoyama's standard
hojicha is a reliable everyday roaster; nothing exceptional but
consistently well-made by a 300+ year house.

name.alternate: 八女今福 深むし茶

producer.name: Haraguchien
producer.history: Fukuoka-based tea wholesaler (茶問屋) founded in 1936 (Showa 11) by a founder originally from Hoshino Village in Yame. Operates as 'Haraguchien Fusōan' (原口園扶桑庵), with shops at Hakata Station, Fukuoka Airport, and major department stores. Vertically integrated: sources from tea gardens, runs its own hi-ire (firing) factory in Yame, and bags in-house. This SKU uses leaves from the Imafuku Tea Cooperative (今福製茶協同組合) in Yame, processed and finished by Haraguchien — a typical Yame fukamushi-cha collaboration between a producing cooperative and a finishing wholesaler.

brewing.notes: Fukamushi (deep-steamed) schedule: 70°C, 30s first; 75°C, 10s second; 80°C, 30s third. Fukamushi-cha needs shorter first-infusion time than asamushi because the deep-steaming has already broken the leaf cells and extraction is fast. Liquor will be cloudy/opaque green — that's normal for fukamushi (vs. the limpid clarity of asamushi). Use a kyusu with a fine mesh filter, since fine particles are part of the style.

notes:
Yame deep-steamed sencha. Fukamushi-cha receives 60-90+ seconds of
steaming (vs. ~30s for asamushi), which breaks down the leaf
structure: shorter brewing time, cloudier/more saturated liquor,
rounder and sweeter on the palate, less astringent than asamushi.
Yame is one of the premier fukamushi-cha regions alongside Kakegawa
in Shizuoka. Haraguchien's signature 'gōgumi' (合組, blending)
technique aims to keep the flavor consistent year-over-year despite
natural harvest variation.

producer.name: Unknown (Yame)
producer.history: Producer not specified. Sourced from the Yame region of Fukuoka prefecture, one of Japan's premier matcha-producing areas. Yame culinary-grade matcha is widely used by Kyushu cafés and confectioners for lattes, sweets, and baking — coarser grind and more astringent than ceremonial grades, but stronger flavor that holds up against milk and sugar.

brewing.notes: Culinary use — not designed for traditional whisking. For matcha latte: 1 tsp (~2g) sifted into a small amount of warm water (60-70°C), whisked into a paste, then add steamed milk. For baking: substitute for cocoa powder ratio in recipes. Storage: refrigerate the bulk bag, transfer small amounts to an airtight container for daily use to limit oxidation. 300g is a lot — open only when ready to use regularly.

notes:
Bulk culinary-grade matcha (300g pouch). Not for ceremonial preparation —
the grind is coarser and the flavor is intentionally stronger and more
astringent to cut through milk, sugar, and dairy fats. Ideal for
matcha lattes, ice cream, baking, smoothies. Once opened, vacuum-seal
small portions or refrigerate in a tight container.
